354	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
355	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
356	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSplitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_shortExact,
357	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeMid, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES,
358	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_splitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι,
359	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι_f_zero, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.mono_horseshoeβ}
360	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
361	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
362	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
363	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
364	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
365	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
366	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
367	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
368	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
369	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
370	\end{lemma}
371	
372	\begin{proof}
373	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp, lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
374	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
375	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
376	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
377	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
378	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
379	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
380	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
381	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
382	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
383	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
384	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
385	\end{proof}

387	\begin{lemma}\leanok
388	[Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer]
389	  \label{lem:quasiIso_tau2}
390	  \lean{HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂}
391	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
392	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
393	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
394	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
395	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
396	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
397	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
398	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
399	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
400	\end{lemma}
401	
402	\begin{proof}
403	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
404	  \leanok
405	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
406	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
407	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
408	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
409	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
410	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
411	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
412	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
413	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
414	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
415	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
416	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
417	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
418	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
419	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
420	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
421	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
422	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
423	\end{proof}

425	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
426	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}
427	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle,
428	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₁₂,
429	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₂₃, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₁,
430	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₂, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₃,
431	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.quasiIso_horseshoeι, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.single₀_hom_ext}
432	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
433	  The cochain complex \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, with the
434	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
435	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
436	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
437	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).
438	\end{lemma}
439	
440	\begin{proof}
441	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
442	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
443	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
444	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
445	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
446	  cochain complexes
447	  \[
448	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
449	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
450	  \]
451	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
452	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
453	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
454	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
455	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
456	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
457	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
458	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
459	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
460	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
461	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
462	\end{proof}

464	\begin{lemma}\leanok
465	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
466	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
467	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
468	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
469	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
470	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
471	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
472	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
473	  \[
474	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
475	  \]
476	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
477	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
478	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
479	  \[
480	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
481	  \]
482	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
483	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
484	  \[
485	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
486	      \longrightarrow 0
487	  \]
488	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
489	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
490	\end{lemma}
491	
492	\begin{proof}
493	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
494	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
495	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
496	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
497	  \leanok
498	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
499	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
500	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
501	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
502	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
503	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
504	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
505	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
506	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
507	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
508	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
509	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
510	\end{proof}

512	\section{The dimension-shift step}
513	
514	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
515	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
516	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
517	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
518	complex-level homology long exact sequence
519	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
520	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
521	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
522	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
523	to objects.
524	
525	\begin{lemma}\leanok
526	[Resolution-level dimension shift]
527	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
528	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES}
529	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
530	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
531	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
532	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
533	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
534	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
535	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
536	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
537	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
538	  \[
539	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
540	  \]
541	\end{lemma}
542	
543	\begin{proof}
544	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
545	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
546	    \leanok
547	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
548	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
549	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
550	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
551	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
552	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
553	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
554	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
555	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
556	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
557	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
558	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
559	\end{proof}
560	
561	\begin{lemma}\leanok
562	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
563	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
564	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
565	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
566	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
567	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
568	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
569	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
570	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
571	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
572	  % between abelian categories and assume
573	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
574	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
575	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
576	  % \begin{enumerate}
577	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
578	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
579	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
580	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
581	  % \end{enumerate}
582	  % In each of the three cases
583	  % $$
584	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
585	  % $$
586	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
587	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
588	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
589	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
590	  \[
591	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
592	  \]
593	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
594	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
595	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
596	  \[
597	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
598	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
599	  \]
600	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
601	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
602	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
603	\end{lemma}
604	
605	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
606	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
607	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
608	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
609	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
611	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
612	\begin{proof}
613	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
614	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
615	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
616	    \leanok
617	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
618	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
619	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
620	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
621	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
622	
623	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
624	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
625	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
626	  complexes
627	  \[
628	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
629	  \]
630	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
631	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
632	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
633	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
634	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
635	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
636	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
637	  \[
638	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
639	      \longrightarrow 0
640	  \]
641	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
642	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
643	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
644	  \[
645	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
646	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
647	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
648	  \]
649	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
650	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
651	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
652	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
653	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
654	  sequence of right-derived functors
655	  \[
656	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
657	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
658	  \]
659	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
660	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
661	  \[
662	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
663	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
664	  \]
665	
666	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
667	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
668	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
669	  \[
670	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
671	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
672	  \]
673	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
674	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
675	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
676	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
677	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
678	\end{proof}
679	
680	\begin{lemma}[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
681	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
682	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}
683	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
684	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
685	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
686	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
687	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
688	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
689	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
690	  % between abelian categories and assume
691	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
692	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
693	  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
694	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
695	  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
696	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
697	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
698	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
699	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
700	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
701	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
702	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
703	  \[
704	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
705	  \]
706	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
707	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
708	  \[
709	    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
710	  \]
711	  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
712	  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
713	\end{lemma}
714	
715	\begin{proof}
716	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
717	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
718	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
719	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
720	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
721	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
722	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
723	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
724	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
725	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
726	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
727	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
728	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
729	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
730	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
731	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
732	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
733	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
734	  initial segment
735	  \[
736	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
737	  \]
738	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
739	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
740	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
741	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
742	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
743	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
744	  which is the assertion.
745	\end{proof}

747	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
748	
749	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
750	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
751	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
752	here.
753	
754	\begin{lemma}\leanok
755	[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
756	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
757	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact}
758	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
759	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
760	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
761	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
762	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
763	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
764	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
765	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
766	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
767	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
768	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
769	  \[
770	    0 \longrightarrow A
771	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
772	      \longrightarrow \cdots
773	  \]
774	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
775	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
776	  \[
777	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
778	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
779	  \]
780	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
781	  \[
782	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
783	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
784	  \]
785	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
786	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
787	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
788	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
789	\end{lemma}
790	
791	\begin{proof}
792	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
793	  \leanok
794	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
795	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
796	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
797	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
798	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
799	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
800	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
801	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
802	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
803	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
804	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
805	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
806	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
807	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
808	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
809	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
810	\end{proof}
811	
812	\begin{lemma}\leanok
813	[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  \leanok
840	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
841	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
842	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
843	  \[
844	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
845	  \]
846	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
847	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
848	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
849	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
850	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
851	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
852	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
853	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
854	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
855	  kernels under precomposition, so
856	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
857	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
858	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
859	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
860	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
861	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
862	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
863	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
864	\end{proof}
865	
866	\begin{lemma}\leanok
867	[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
868	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
869	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
870	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
871	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
872	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
873	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
874	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
875	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
876	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
877	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
878	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
879	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
880	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
881	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
882	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
883	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
884	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
885	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
886	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
887	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
888	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
889	  \[
890	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
891	    \qquad
892	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
893	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
894	  \]
895	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
896	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
897	\end{lemma}
898	
899	\begin{proof}
900	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
901	  \leanok
902	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
903	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
904	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
905	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
906	  \[
907	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
908	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
909	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
910	  \]
911	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
912	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
913	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
914	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
915	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
916	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
917	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
918	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
919	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
920	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
921	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
922	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
923	\end{proof}

925	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
926	
927	\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
928	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
929	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
930	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
931	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
932	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
933	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
934	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
935	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
936	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
937	  % L5785--5811).
938	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
939	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
940	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
941	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
942	  % $$
943	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
944	  % $$
945	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
946	  % $RF$."
947	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
948	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
949	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
950	  % and we obtain an exact functor
951	  % $$
952	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
953	  % $$
954	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
955	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
956	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
957	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
958	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
959	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
960	  Let
961	  \[
962	    0 \longrightarrow A
963	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
964	      \longrightarrow \cdots
965	  \]
966	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
967	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
968	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
969	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
970	  \[
971	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
972	  \]
973	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
974	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
975	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
976	  object.
977	\end{theorem}
978	
979	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
980	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
981	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
982	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
983	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
984	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
985	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
986	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
987	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
988	% the long exact cohomology sequence
989	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
990	% to an exact sequence
991	% $$
992	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
993	% $$
994	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
995	\begin{proof}
996	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
997	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
998	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
999	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1000	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1001	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1002	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1003	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
1004	
1005	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1006	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1007	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1008	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1009	
1010	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1011	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1012	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1013	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1014	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1015	  \[
1016	    (R^n G)(A)
1017	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1018	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1019	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1020	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1021	  \]
1022	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1023	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1024	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1025	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1026	  acyclic:
1027	  \[
1028	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1029	  \]
1030	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1031	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1032	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1033	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1034	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1035	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1036	
1037	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
1038	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
1039	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
1040	  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
1041	  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
1042	  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
1043	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
1044	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
1045	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
1046	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
1047	\end{proof}
1048

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
20	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
21	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
22	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
23	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
24	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
25	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
26	
27	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
28	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
29	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
30	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
31	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
32	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
33	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
34	\[
35	  \begin{array}{ccc}
36	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
37	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
38	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
39	  \end{array}
40	\]
41	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
42	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
43	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
44	
45	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
46	
47	\begin{definition}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
51	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
53	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
54	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
55	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
56	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
57	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
58	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
59	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
60	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
61	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
62	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
63	  % the unit ideal, see
64	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
65	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
66	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
67	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
68	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
69	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
70	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
71	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
72	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
73	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
74	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
75	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
76	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
77	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
78	  \[
79	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
80	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
81	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
82	  \]
83	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
84	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
85	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
86	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
87	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
88	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
89	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
90	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
91	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
92	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
93	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
94	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
95	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
96	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
97	\end{definition}
98	
99	\begin{definition}
100	
101	\leanok
102	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
103	  \label{def:cech_complex}
104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
105	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
107	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
108	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
109	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
110	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
111	  % is identified with the complex
112	  % $$
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
116	  % \ldots
117	  % $$
118	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
119	  % \begin{equation}
120	  % \label{equation-extended}
121	  % 0 \to
122	  % M \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
126	  % \ldots
127	  % \end{equation}
128	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
129	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
130	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
131	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
132	  vanishing).}
133	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
134	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
135	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
136	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
137	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
138	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
139	  in degree \(p\) it is
140	  \[
141	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
142	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
143	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
144	  \]
145	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
146	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
147	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
148	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
149	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
150	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
151	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
152	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
153	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
154	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
155	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
156	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
157	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
158	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
159	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
160	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
161	\end{definition}
162	
163	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
164	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
165	
166	It is convenient to factor the construction of
167	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
168	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the entire mathematical
169	content that is not yet available off the shelf is isolated in the middle piece.
170	
171	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
172	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
173	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
174	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
175	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
176	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
177	\[
178	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
179	    \;=\;
180	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
181	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
182	\]
183	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
184	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
185	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
186	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
187	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
188	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
189	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
190	
191	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
192	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
193	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
194	\[
195	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
196	  \qquad
197	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
198	\]
199	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
200	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
201	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
202	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
203	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
204	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
205	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
206	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
207	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
208	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
209	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
210	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
211	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
212	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
213	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
214	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. This
215	functoriality is \emph{not} coupled to the project's tensor--pullback substrate;
216	it consumes only Mathlib's pseudofunctor packaging of pullback and pushforward.
217	Concretely, pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
218	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
219	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
220	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
222	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts about this
223	packaging: the mate identity expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback
224	comparison as the pushforward comparison (the lemma
225	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}), its identity normalisation
226	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}, and the pseudofunctor unitor and pentagon
227	(associativity) coherences \texttt{pseudofunctor\_left\_unitality},
228	\texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}.
229	All of these are already available off the shelf, so the functoriality of the
230	relative-direct-image functor is a \emph{consumer of Mathlib's coherence},
231	independent of the project-local sheafification/pullback machinery of the
232	tensor--pullback substrate. The laws are nonetheless not formal trivialities: each
233	is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison isomorphisms along
234	the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and past the adjunction
235	unit, gluing them across two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions; the obstacle is the
236	bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence supplied by
254	Mathlib (the comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities), not any project-local ingredient.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}\leanok
277	[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
278	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve}
280	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
281	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
282	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
283	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
284	  \[
285	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
286	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
287	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
288	  \]
289	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
290	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
291	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
292	\end{definition}
293	
294	\begin{definition}\leanok
295	[Push--pull object map]
296	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
298	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
299	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
300	  \[
301	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
302	  \]
303	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
304	\end{definition}
305	
306	\begin{definition}\leanok
307	[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
332	\end{definition}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
339	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	  A statement purely about the pushforward/pullback comparison isomorphisms, with no
345	  sectionwise content. Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward
346	  comparison as the conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison
347	  (\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}); the identity leg then collapses by the
348	  identity normalisation \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom} together with the right
349	  unitality \texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, and the two over-triangle
350	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
373	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
374	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
375	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
376	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
377	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
378	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
379	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
380	\end{proof}
381	
382	\begin{lemma}
383	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
384	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
386	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
387	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
388	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
389	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
390	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
391	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
392	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
393	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
394	  \[
395	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
396	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
397	    \;=\;
398	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
399	  \]
400	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
401	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
402	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
403	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
404	\end{lemma}
405	
406	\begin{proof}
407	  Proved directly in Lean.
408	\end{proof}
409	
410	\begin{lemma}
411	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
412	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
413	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
414	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
415	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
416	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
417	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
418	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
419	  collapses to the transport-light form
420	  \[
421	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
422	      \;\circ\; e_2
423	    \;=\;
424	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
425	      \;\circ\; e_3,
426	  \]
427	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
428	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
429	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
430	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
431	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
432	  composition (pentagon) law.
433	\end{lemma}
434	
435	\begin{proof}
436	  Proved directly in Lean.
437	\end{proof}
438	
439	\begin{definition}
440	[Push--pull functor]
441	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
443	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
444	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
445	  \[
446	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
447	  \]
448	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
449	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
450	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
451	  laws established above: the identity law
452	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
453	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
454	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
455	\end{definition}
456	
457	\begin{definition}
458	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
459	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
460	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
461	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
462	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
463	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of
464	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} through the push--pull functor
465	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the
466	  product over \((i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}\) of the \((p+1)\)-fold-intersection
467	  pushforwards \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\). This is the
468	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
469	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to
470	  \mathcal{C}^0\).
471	\end{definition}
472	
473	\begin{definition}\leanok
474	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
475	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
476	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
477	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
478	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
479	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
480	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
481	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
482	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
483	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
484	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
485	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
486	\end{definition}

488	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
489	
490	\begin{lemma}
491	
492	\leanok
493	[Standard-cover {\v C}ech-complex vanishing on affines]
494	  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
495	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
496	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
497	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
498	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
499	  % {\v C}ech vanishing) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52).
500	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
501	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
502	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
503	  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
504	  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
505	  % all $p > 0$."
506	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
507	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
508	  vanishing).}
509	  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
510	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
511	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
512	  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
513	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
514	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} has vanishing cohomology in all positive
515	  degrees:
516	  \[
517	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
518	  \]
519	  Equivalently, the extended complex
520	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}}
521	  \to \cdots\) of localisations is exact. This is the standard-cover {\v C}ech
522	  vanishing only; the sheaf-cohomology Serre vanishing
523	  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) is the separate
524	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} below, obtained from this complex
525	  vanishing by the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.
526	\end{lemma}
527	
528	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
529	% In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
530	% which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
531	% Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
532	% ...
533	% It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
534	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
535	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
536	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
537	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
538	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
539	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...
540	% Let us define a homotopy
541	% $$
542	% h :
543	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
544	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
545	% \longrightarrow
546	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
547	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
548	% $$
549	% by the rule
550	% $$
551	% h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
552	% $$
553	% ... We compute ... $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ ...
554	% This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired."
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
557	  Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) for an
558	  \(A\)-module \(M\). By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
559	  the standard cover is the complex of localisations
560	  \[
561	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
562	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
563	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
564	  \]
565	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
566	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
567	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}).
568	  Exactness may be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime
569	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\) (Algebra, Lemma
570	  \texttt{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}). Choose an index
571	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
572	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
573	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
574	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
575	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
576	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
577	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
578	  \(p > 0\).
579	\end{proof}

581	\begin{lemma}
582	[Serre vanishing on affines]
583	  \label{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}
584	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_serre_vanishing}
585	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}
586	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG, Lemma
587	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on
588	  % affines) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L145--155).
589	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
590	  % for quasi-coherent modules.
591	  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
592	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
593	  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
594	  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
595	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG
596	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
597	  affines).}
598	  Let \(U\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
599	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. Then the sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) vanishes in
600	  all positive degrees:
601	  \[
602	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
603	  \]
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We are going to apply
607	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
608	% As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
609	% the affine opens of $X$.
610	% As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
611	% open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
612	% Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
613	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
614	% hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
615	% another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
616	% The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
617	% of $\mathcal{B}$, see
618	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.
619	% Hence (2) holds.
620	% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
621	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
622	\begin{proof}
623	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}
624	  Take the affine opens of \(U\) as a basis \(\mathcal{B}\) of the topology and the
625	  standard open covers of affine opens as the admissible covers. The intersection of
626	  two standard opens in an affine is again a standard open, so \(\mathcal{B}\) is
627	  closed under the relevant intersections; and the standard covers form a cofinal
628	  system among the open covers of any basic open. The standard-cover {\v C}ech
629	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} supplies the acyclicity
630	  hypothesis. The {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis
631	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) then identifies the sheaf cohomology
632	  of \(\mathcal{F}\) on each basic open with its {\v C}ech cohomology for a standard
633	  cover, whence \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
634	\end{proof}

673	  % hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
674	  % another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.

681	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Lemma
682	  \texttt{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (as applied in the proof of
683	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}).}
684	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a basis for its topology, and let a
685	  set of admissible open covers be given, subject to: (1) \(\mathcal{B}\) is closed
686	  under finite intersection; (2) for each \(B \in \mathcal{B}\) the admissible covers
687	  of \(B\) are cofinal among all open covers of \(B\); and (3) for every admissible
688	  cover \(\mathcal{U}\) of every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\), every quasi-coherent

690	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Then for every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\)
691	  and every \(p\) the canonical comparison map is an isomorphism

693	  under (3), \(H^p(B, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). This is the

699	\begin{proof}
700	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
701	  General sheaf theory: under (1)--(3), the {\v C}ech-to-derived-functor spectral
702	  sequence for a cover \(\mathcal{U}\) of \(B \in \mathcal{B}\) has its higher rows
703	  killed by hypothesis (3) applied to the elements of \(\mathcal{B}\) appearing as
704	  the finite intersections of \(\mathcal{U}\) (these lie in \(\mathcal{B}\) by (1)),
705	  so it collapses and the edge map \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
706	  H^p(B, \mathcal{F})\) is an isomorphism; cofinality (2) makes the identification
707	  independent of the chosen admissible cover. This is the standard Čech-comparison
708	  criterion on a basis, applied with \(\mathcal{B}\) the affine opens and the
709	  admissible covers the standard covers, condition (3) being the standard-cover
710	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. \emph{The general
711	  basis-comparison criterion for sheaves of modules on a scheme is not yet available
712	  in Mathlib and is recorded here as a to-build dependency.}
713	\end{proof}
